title: My First Post

content: Hello, world!This is the first post on my blog. Markdown is pleasantly simple: this is a list this is also a list this is a quote this is one line of code
title: My First Post

content: A short note for tool lovers: the real value is not how many tools you collect, but whether you can keep the methods they taught you.
title: A Bigger Toolbox Is Not the Goal. Methods Are.

content: It is easy to become obsessed with finding better tools. A new editor, a new AI product, or a new productivity system always makes it feel like peak efficiency is one more migration away. But what creates long-term value is usually not the tool itself. It is whether you kept the method behind the tool: why this tool fits a certain kind of task what it actually saves compared with the previous one where it breaks down how you should judge similar situations next time If none of that gets written down, every tool change feels like starting over. That is why I want this blog to slowly become a repository of methods: tools can be replaced workflows can evolve but judgment and method should become more stable over time That is one of the reasons this site exists.
title: A Bigger Toolbox Is Not the Goal. Methods Are.

content: A longer sample post for testing table of contents, long paragraphs, lists, and blockquotes.
title: Building a Writing System Matters More Than Finding the Next Tool

content: Why people stop writingOften it is not because they lack ideas, but because they lack a stable writing system. We tend to blame the tools: the Markdown editor is not good enough the publishing flow is annoying the theme does not feel right the tag system is still unclear Those things do affect the experience, but they are rarely the deciding factor. What usually makes writing hard to sustain is the lack of a workflow that is light enough, stable enough, and reusable enough.
title: Building a Writing System Matters More Than Finding the Next Tool

content: A sustainable minimum loopThe loop I trust more now is simple: capture scattered ideas during daily work regularly group those fragments into themes write short posts instead of trying to finish something huge in one go publish first and allow future revision The benefit is that it does not force you into a “this must be complete now” mindset every time you write.
title: Building a Writing System Matters More Than Finding the Next Tool

content: Why blogs still matterSocial platforms are faster, but blogs are better for accumulation. One major advantage of a blog is this: You can build a context of your own instead of living inside a platform’s feed ranking forever. Writing a blog does not mean rejecting every other platform. It means keeping one place that you can revisit for years.
title: Building a Writing System Matters More Than Finding the Next Tool

content: A simple rule of thumbIf a piece of writing is still worth reopening after one week, it deserves a chance to enter the blog. If it still helps you recover a judgment, a method, or the context of a problem after one month, it deserves deeper systematization.
title: Building a Writing System Matters More Than Finding the Next Tool

content: ClosingTools will keep changing, but a good writing system lets you continue producing through those changes.
title: Building a Writing System Matters More Than Finding the Next Tool

content: A sample post for testing code blocks, monospace fonts, and configuration snippets.
title: A Few Hugo Snippets That Are Enough to Keep This Blog Growing

content: Homepage post listSometimes you only want posts under the blog section: go-html-template {{ $posts := where site.RegularPages "Section" "blog" }} {{ range first 5 $posts }} {{ .Title }} {{ end }}
title: A Few Hugo Snippets That Are Enough to Keep This Blog Growing

content: Tag linksTurn tags into clickable links: go-html-template {{ range .Params.tags }} {{ . }} {{ end }}
title: A Few Hugo Snippets That Are Enough to Keep This Blog Growing

content: A minimal menu in config toml [[main]] name = "Posts" url = "/blog/" weight = 20
title: A Few Hugo Snippets That Are Enough to Keep This Blog Growing

content: Why keep these snippetsBecause a reusable snippet is often more valuable in the long run than searching for the same answer again.
title: A Few Hugo Snippets That Are Enough to Keep This Blog Growing

content: A short sample post used to test homepage lists, archives, and tag pages with multiple entries.
title: Reading Notes Do Not Need to Be Complete. Leave Searchable Anchors First.

content: Reading notes often become a burden because we want every one of them to be a complete summary. For long-term accumulation, a more realistic approach is to keep anchors first: which chapter is worth returning to which concept connects with the project at hand which sentence changed your judgment Those searchable anchors are often much easier to sustain than trying to finish a polished long-form summary in one sitting.
title: Reading Notes Do Not Need to Be Complete. Leave Searchable Anchors First.

content: Why this blog exists: a place for programming notes, tools, workflows, and methods that stay useful over time.
title: About

content: I am ZenGeekLabs. This site is where I keep notes on programming, tools, knowledge management, and the experiments worth revisiting. Over time, it should grow into both a personal blog and a digital garden: write down technical notes and practical lessons archive useful tool experience keep methods that can be reused for a long time It does not try to put a self-introduction first. I would rather let the work and the writing speak first. If you also spend time exploring code, tools, and workflows, or you are looking for a writing system that supports long-term accumulation, there may be something here for you.
title: About
